name: Victor Young
description: American composer, arranger, violinist and conductor, orchestra leader (1900-1956)
abstract: Albert Victor Young (August 8, 1899– November 10, 1956)  was an American composer, arranger, violinist and conductor. Young was posthumously awarded the Academy Award for Best Music Score of a Dramatic or Comedy Picture for Around the World in 80 Days at the 29th Academy Awards in 1957.

works.t: Mazurka No. 2 in B-flat Major, Op. 54

about_quotes.speaker: Big Chielf Moore
about_quotes.evidence: The mention of Victor Young is made by the [INTERVIEWER], not the [INTERVIEWEE]. The [INTERVIEWEE] does not respond with any personal connection or confirmation of meeting him.

about_quotes.speaker: Skitch Henderson
about_quotes.evidence: I watched men like Victor Young, I’ll never forget, we played a main title for Cecil B. DeMille and Victor was a magnificent orchestrator... I was on the date.
about_quotes.doc_title: Skitch Henderson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New Milford, Connecticut, October 31, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Keith Ingham
about_quotes.evidence: Keith has created memorable albums devoted to the music of Fred Astaire, Billy Strayhorn, Fats Waller and Victor Young.
about_quotes.doc_title: Keith Ingham interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Bucky Pizzarelli
about_quotes.evidence: I’m playing things that I used to listen to on record, a score by Victor Young called “For Whom The Bell Tolls.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Bucky Pizzarelli interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Chauncey Morehouse
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Now did you make records with Victor Young and the Brunswick orchestra? [INTERVIEWEE] Yes, many.

about_quotes.speaker: Quincy Jones
about_quotes.evidence: Then I could hear Victor Young at Paramount. They became like ideologies That was the bar – the standard.
about_quotes.doc_title: Quincy Jones — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
